item.title: Re: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Replying on behalf of N_Aepic_Fael

We have sourced a better link that includes all three images currently being used. This is shown here: http://www.vaulot.com/portfolio/borabora/.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Posting here for clarity.

Although the old link's domain has expired, Vaulot & Dyèvre do own the images, such a shown here: https://anti-utopias.com/newswire/body-exhibition/.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nearly 2020 and that inconsistency is still here.
It looks like the OP set it in France solely because the original artwork was in French but somehow didn't felt the need to apply this location to the rest of the article.

Not only that, but whatever is written on the boxes and the literature found with the pills should probably be mentioned to be in French quoted as a translation rather than a straight copy of it, although this is more plausible and less ludicrous than it being found during a drug raid operated by a foreign organization or mentioning that the box doesn't include a sign used by the government of another country - it probably doesn't carry signs used by the Japanese government either while we are at it.

It's disappointing because it distracts from the anomalous part. I suspend my vote until a rework is done on this one, even though the OP is now unlikely to do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's unlikely that ''Lumen" and "K" refer to the names of active ingredients because they already refer to units of measurement.
Lumen is used for the amount of light and Kelvin is used for color temperature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sunlight pills, now you can be as grossly incandescent as the sun itself!

Anyway with that out the way. I like this, it's got a decent idea with (personally) really good execution. I myself didn't find any issues with it but I'm not the best at doing that kinda thing.

Upvote from me for certain, it's fun to find little things like this that I've missed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I too logged in just to mention this. It's what made me cancel my upvote on the second reading. (Don't know how I missed that the first time.)
As much as Americans want to be the world police, they'd not be the ones to handle this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Taste the sun

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"various suppositories"

Now you can stick it up where it don't shine, and make it shine!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to say, this is a great example of taking a cool-looking picture, and building an awesome skip around it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it. The creativity is off the wall. Really captured my attention.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I smirked a little when I saw:

vaginal rings

This wasn't enough to make me upvote though.
I have no problem with this ship, it just doesn't entertain me very much.
I also agree that the Foundation wouldn't prescribe these things to people until it had done some more research, and found out how to at least make them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't even have any good ideas for Safe objects, so I suspect that they are probably harder for me to write.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You made an interesting Safe object, which can really be harder than writing an interesting Euclid or Keter one.

Not necessarily. I've found that I tend to see a lot of Euclid and Keter drafts that aren't interesting, since most of them are generic monsters or powered-up humanoids. Safe objects allow for a different sort of peek into the Foundation'verse since there's more leeway for non-dangerous experimental tests.

Of course, I'm likely biased since almost all my SCP articles are Safe class, and I've never written a Keter SCP. d:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*Solaire Memes*
But seriously, good job. You made an interesting Safe object, which can really be harder than writing an interesting Euclid or Keter one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose. I figured this was supposed to imply that they were still visible on EM, UV, IR spectrums, what have you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to post exactly this. I know how weird it feels to say this in a site full of weird stuff as this, but it breaks disbelief when not only the DEA steps in (which doesn't happen unless they had an open investigation going on and they believed the French police couldn't be trusted) but several standards mentioned are US-based when this is clearly a company based in Europe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have a lot of american terminology (FDA/DEA) but it says these were found in france. France would have different enforcement agencies than the US. It would greatly improve authenticity if you could find equivalent french agencies, and fix those references, or simply change it to having been found in the US. Otherwise, a solid SCP. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I once held a RL Biochem research job assisting with developing drug candidates from molecules that are -almost- the same shape as Vitamin D. Lots of rats were involved.
You could say that this SCP breaks all prior records at being relevant to my interests.

Two things I felt I should mention:
Female rats in RL model osteoporosis (bone loss) in almost identical ways to humans, proportionally speaking. This includes modeling the effects of drug candidates. If somebody were to add experiment log content for this SCP, I would NOT expect D-class to be involved at all until -extremely- late stage research (double-checking efficacy on humans) Rats are more appropriate, cheaper, and overall easier to work with.

Hypervitaminosis D (Vitamin D overdose) is listed by name in side effects paragraph, than the actual symptoms of Vitamin D overdose are listed as well, mixed in with the paranormal effects. Congratulations on doing your research, but isn't that redundant writing?

There are two ways you could choose to fix this:
1. Remove the term Hypervitaminosis D. Just list the symptoms, both real and paranormal, all mixed together like they are and leave the actual title of the mundane problems out of the article.
2. Split the symptom lists into two portions; one paranormal and one mundane. Directly explain the mundane symptoms as being typical for Vitamin D overdose.
I suppose it's a matter of which method fits the style / tone for the article better.
Oh boy! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could you reproduce this using SCP-038? If you could, that would be amazing! If it would work properly, that would be like unlimited relaxation!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't overthink about it. It will hurt your brain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While some people may frown on the fact that this SCP is played straight from an artwork, I actually like this one because of that fact. Of course, some creativity is needed to fill in some missing details to make it more SCP-appropriate, and I like how (within the SCP universe) it is implied that these pills are the work of a highly-organized and professional but possibly illegal pharmaceutical company who made them using some unexplained anomalous means. The use of medical terms and jargon makes it the more believable.

And that side effects/symptom list made me laugh. It's almost like a parody of those long list of side effects in medicine advertisements.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bio-luminescence and invisibility …

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The most interesting part as I see it is that bioluminescence is a symptom of overdose, yet that isn't the one that gets redacted. Makes you wonder how crazy the redacted detail must be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like most of it rather a lot, but I must say I hate lists that end with redaction. It reads to me like the author ran out of ideas before coming up with the actually really interesting bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
invisibility from the visible spectrum - Is this right? Shouldn't it just say invisible?

Specifying that the invisibility is from the visual spectrum seems redundant.

And speaking of redundancy…what about those two addendum? Unless I'm missing something, they don't really seem to add anything to this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this SCP, nice to see that not everything the Foundation finds is pure condensed terror but can still be mind-bendingly bizarre.

I'm a bit confused on why the manufacturers of this drug had to skulk away through secret tunnels to escape police raids though? Physics defying they may be, but the drugs appears to be a perfectly safe and non-addictive, produced by a legitimate pharmaceutical company and apparently intended to be sold at market. So why does Sunset Lab act like criminals producing narcotics? Is there some nefarious secret to their production that justifies the police raid and the coverup by the company that owned the lab?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can't move posts to the top. Also, there was a 30 minute gap between their post and your posts. Just try to post the image's source asap or beforehand next time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know how to put my comments at the top. Someone posted here first before me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm, this is a problem too. It's literally what comes to mind when you say "sunlight pills".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm conflicted here. I really, really like this SCP, but it's literally exactly what the original piece of artwork was, down to using the same warning label. And yes, I understand that you received permission from the original artists, which is good, but I'm not sure I can get over the fact that this isn't really original work. I mean, it's not plagiarism, exactly, but… gah.

No vote for now, while I ponder this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The term 'abusive' is slightly tone-breaking, though. Normally, in the US, ads would say, "Use only as directed".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"All rights reserved" usually means that it's under a standard copyright. Did you get permission from the photographer to use these photos?

Never mind, it helps to read further down the article. In the future, however, you might want to ensure that your license disclosure notice is at the top of the discussion thread. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting idea, but it takes waaaaaay too long to get it across. I would upvote this if it were around half the length.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be fair, it was a quote taken directly from the site of the original artists, who happen to be French. There is a difference between abuse and exceeding your recommended dosage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Saying "abusive use" at the end of that last note's kinda redundant because abuse already implies excessive use of the material in question.

Besides that, there were some bits that didn't quite click for me, but overall +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sunlight pills! I couldn't see anything wrong with this and I actually quite liked this. Though I was wondering what happened in the Incident 2202-SOLAR-13, I guess that would defeat the purpose of leaving it to the imagination.

+1

E: I guess you could say that this SCP brightened up my day?! /shot
I'm not even sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with Zolgamax; it was interesting to have to read until the near- end for the "pay-off", so to speak.

And oh man, those overdose effects. I mean, imagine a commercial for this pill! Seriously, those effects were equal doses disturbing and amusing, especially with the [REDACTED] at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I left in those anomalous effects since they were effects that could possibly cause containment breaches, the expunged stuff is much more dangerous and potentially physic breaking, in theory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I was sort of surprised when I looked up "solar elastosis" and found that it was a perfectly boring actual condition which actually exists. I was especially surprised when "polymorphous light eruption" also turned out to be a thing, a thing that does not involve tangible light coming out of any orifices or anything.
I would've left at least one undefined anomalous effect outside of the [DATA EXPUNGED] personally, but I still like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The info about how they were found IS in an addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really neat, but I highly suggest moving the info about how these were found into its own addendum. There's too much information between "this is what it is" and "this is what it does".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Photo credits: Vaulot & Dyèvre. All rights reserved. Photo Credit: Vaulot & Dyèvre. All rights reserved.

http://www.vaulotdyevre.com/VaulotDyevre_Miniflux_HD.html

I liked to thank Vaulot & Dyèvre for granting me permission to use their artwork, everyone in #Site19, Dr.ClefLucifer, Cthulu, Bird Jesus, Our Lord and Savior Helix, and most importantly, myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks !

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://www.vaulotdyevre.com/VaulotDyevre_Miniflux_HD.html

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They did make sure to thoroughly test the drug after a long period of time, I felt that after confirming its safe usage (when taken properly), it would warrant its beneficial use.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Photo credits: Vaulot & Dyèvre. All rights reserved. Photo Credit: Vaulot & Dyèvre. All rights reserved.

http://www.vaulotdyevre.com/VaulotDyevre_Miniflux_HD.html

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, shrink those images here or something, one of them is 10 MB.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You still have some XXXXs in there.

I question whether the Foundation would prescribe a drug that they do not fully understand nor can reproduce to personnel. Staff taking a mystery drug is unnecessary and dangerous, and the idea that medical staff would prescribe it is rather ridiculous. I feel they would be much more cautious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where was this image from? Just asking curiously :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Side effects may include…

Yeah, that paragraph made it for me.

I really like this one for another, more important reason, though. It has a unique flavor: tedium. Which isn't to say that reading this article is a tedious experience. Rather, the article conveys the idea that testing this article was a long, drawn-out process, during which not much happened. It's nice to see that things like this happen at the Foundation, rather than every object bringing with it guaranteed terror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upload these images locally. Off-site images aren't allowed here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That last image is waaaay too big.

You should probably resize it.
